items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: What does Maslow's hierarchy of needs propose about motivation?
items.correct: Humans are motivated by a hierarchy of needs (physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualisation) — lower needs must be substantially met before higher ones motivate.
items.misconception: Maslow said money is the most important motivator at work.
items.random_wrong: Maslow said employees must be promoted every year.
items.why: Students conflate Maslow with Taylor (who DID emphasise pay). Maslow's whole point is that once basic needs are met, higher needs (recognition, self-actualisation) motivate — not more money.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary market research?
items.correct: Primary research is original data the business gathers itself (surveys, interviews); secondary research uses existing data (reports, government statistics, market intelligence).
items.misconception: Primary research is the most important and secondary research is less important.
items.random_wrong: Primary research is done first and secondary research is done later.
items.why: Primary and secondary refer to data SOURCE, not importance or order. Secondary research is often done first (it's cheaper). Both are valuable for different reasons.

items.q: Schumpeter described entrepreneurs as agents of:
items.why: It's Creative destruction. “Marginal utility” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of UK start-ups fail within three years?
items.why: Correct answer: 60%. “20%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Dyson launch his bagless cyclone vacuum?
items.why: The real figure is 1993 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
